title: How to Spot Failed Payment Churn Before It Kills MRR
meta_description: Failed payments silently drain 5-15% of SaaS MRR. Here's how to catch involuntary churn fast, before it shows up in your monthly numbers.
h1: The Fastest Way to Spot Failed Payment Churn Before It Tanks Your SaaS Revenue
body_markdown:
You open Stripe on a Tuesday morning. Three customers you thought were paying you $99/month haven't actually paid in 21 days. Their cards failed, Stripe retried, Stripe gave up, and now they're either about to cancel or already gone — and you had no idea.

This is involuntary churn, and for most SaaS companies between $1k and $50k MRR, it's silently eating 5% to 15% of revenue every month. Profitwell's data on subscription payment failures puts the number around 9% on average. At $20k MRR, that's $1,800 a month walking out the door because a card expired or a bank rejected a charge.

The frustrating part: most of this is recoverable. You just have to see it fast enough to act.

## Why Failed Payment Churn Is Different From Regular Churn

When a customer actively cancels, you get a signal. They click a button, you get an email, maybe they even tell you why. You can follow up, offer a discount, learn something.

Failed payment churn — also called involuntary churn or delinquent churn — has no signal. The customer didn't decide to leave. Their card hit its expiration date. Their bank flagged the recurring charge as suspicious. The charge exceeded a daily limit. They got a new card after a fraud alert and never updated it.

By the time Stripe marks the subscription as `past_due` or `unpaid`, you've usually already lost three things:

1. **The payment itself** (often the easiest to recover).
2. **The customer relationship** (they stopped using the product the day access got restricted).
3. **The chance to fix it quietly** (now they remember their card failed, which makes "maybe I don't need this" a much easier thought).

The window between "card failed" and "customer is gone forever" is short. Usually 5 to 10 days. Miss it and the recovery rate drops hard.

## The Real Numbers Behind a Stripe Failed Payment

Here's what actually happens when a charge fails on a recurring Stripe subscription:

- **Day 0:** Charge fails. Stripe schedules a retry based on your Smart Retries or custom retry schedule.
- **Day 3-5:** First retry. If this fails, Stripe sends another customer email (if you have that on).
- **Day 7-14:** Additional retries. Subscription status moves to `past_due`.
- **Day 14-21:** Stripe gives up. Subscription moves to `unpaid` or `canceled` depending on your settings.

In theory, Stripe handles this for you. In practice, here's what breaks:

- Your customer email settings are off, or the emails go to spam.
- The customer sees a "your card failed" email and ignores it because it looks like phishing.
- The retry schedule is too aggressive and the bank blocks every attempt.
- You never personally reach out, because you don't know it happened.

The last one is the killer. Every SaaS founder I know who's actively watching failed payments recovers 40-70% of them with a single personal email. The ones who rely entirely on Stripe's automated dunning recover closer to 15-25%.

## How to Actually Spot This Early

Forget the weekly check-in on the Stripe dashboard. You need something closer to real-time. Here's a tiered approach depending on how much infrastructure you want to build.

### Level 1: Stripe Webhooks to Slack or Email

Set up a webhook listener for these events:

- `invoice.payment_failed`
- `customer.subscription.past_due`
- `charge.failed` (for recurring charges)

Pipe them into a Slack channel or a dedicated email inbox. The minute a payment fails, you see it. You don't wait for Stripe's retry cycle to finish before you know there's a problem.

This takes maybe two hours to set up with a simple Node or Python endpoint, or about 15 minutes with a no-code tool like Zapier or Make. The downside: you'll get noisy. Every failure, including ones that will self-recover on the next retry, shows up.

### Level 2: A Filtered Daily Digest

Instead of every event, pull a daily report of:

- Subscriptions that entered `past_due` in the last 24 hours
- Invoices with 2+ failed attempts
- Any customer with lifetime value over $X whose latest charge failed

This cuts the noise and lets you prioritize. A customer paying you $500/month getting a failed charge is a very different problem from a $19/month trial conversion failing.

You can build this with the Stripe API — query `invoices` with `status=open` and `attempt_count >= 2`, join against `customers`, and sort by subscription value.

### Level 3: Watch the Leading Indicator — Card Expirations

The absolute fastest way to spot failed payment churn is to catch it before it happens. Query your Stripe data for cards expiring in the next 30-60 days. For each one, check whether there's been a card update recently. If not, send a personal note: "Hey, your card on file expires next month, want to update it before things break?"

This single workflow has the highest ROI of anything in this post. Companies that run it consistently see involuntary churn drop by 30-50%.

## What to Do the Moment You See a Failed Payment

When a high-value charge fails, the clock starts. Here's a template that works surprisingly well:

> Subject: Your card didn't go through — no rush, just a heads up
> 
> Hey [Name] — Stripe flagged that your last payment for [Product] didn't clear. Happens all the time, usually just an expired card or a fraud alert from the bank. If you can update it here [link], I'll make sure you don't lose access. Let me know if anything weird is going on and I'll sort it out manually.

Three things make this work:

1. **It sounds like a person**, not a system. It probably won't get filtered.
2. **It removes blame.** The customer isn't embarrassed. "Happens all the time."
3. **It offers a human fallback.** Some people just want to tell you what's up.

Send it within 24-48 hours of the first failure, not after Stripe has given up.

## Metrics to Track Weekly

If you're not measuring involuntary churn separately from voluntary churn, start now. You want:

- **Failed payment rate**: % of charges that fail on first attempt.
- **Recovery rate**: % of failed payments that eventually succeed (through retries, manual outreach, or card updates).
- **Involuntary churn rate**: customers lost purely due to payment failure, not a cancellation decision.
- **MRR at risk**: total monthly revenue from subscriptions currently in `past_due` or with a recent failed payment.

That last one is the number that should live on a dashboard you look at daily. Not monthly. Not when you remember. Daily.

## The Honest Problem

Most solo founders and two-person teams know all this in theory. The problem isn't knowledge — it's that logging into Stripe, filtering, exporting, and cross-referencing takes 20 minutes that you don't have when you're also shipping product, doing support, and writing marketing copy.

This is the gap tools like [Stripe Revenue Tracker](#) fill — pulling real MRR, failed payments, and at-risk subscriptions into one view you can check in 30 seconds without digging through the Stripe dashboard. The value isn't the data itself; it's seeing the right number at the right time without it becoming another task on your list.

## The Bottom Line

Failed payment churn looks like a small problem on any given day. One card. Maybe $49. But it compounds. If you're losing 9% of MRR to it every month and recovering none of it, you're working twice as hard on acquisition to stand still.

The founders who solve this don't do anything clever. They just see failed payments the day they happen, send a personal note, and keep a close eye on cards that are about to expire. That's the whole playbook. The trick is having a system where "seeing it" takes seconds, not a Tuesday-morning detective session in the Stripe dashboard.
